title: The Operations Audit for Manufacturing Companies Under 200 Employees
subtitle: Map where your team bleeds time, money, and sanity before spending a dollar on new software or automation

sections.title: Order Intake and Job Routing
sections.description: Where most manufacturing companies lose time without realizing it. Audit how orders enter, get assigned, and move before work actually starts.

items.text: Consolidate order intake into one consistent channel instead of scattered email, phone, text, and walk-ins
items.tip: Pick the channel your biggest customers already prefer and route everything else into it within 30 days

items.text: Log every order in one system of record before any work begins
items.tip: Kill verbal instructions and sticky notes. If it is not in the system, it does not exist

items.text: Route jobs to the correct department, machine, or team using documented rules, not tribal knowledge
items.tip: Write the routing logic on paper first. If one person leaving breaks it, you have a risk, not a process

items.text: Document how many times a single order is manually re-entered or copied between systems
items.tip: Walk one live order from intake to shipping and count every keystroke of duplicated data

sections.title: Production Tracking and Floor Visibility
sections.description: If you cannot see the floor in real time, you are managing by gut feel. That breaks around 50 employees and never recovers on its own.

items.text: Display a live view of jobs in progress, queued, and blocked on a digital or physical board
items.tip: A whiteboard updated hourly beats a fancy dashboard updated weekly

items.text: Require operators to log job status changes as they happen, not at end of shift
items.tip: Put the logging device within 10 feet of the machine or the data will always be late

items.text: Track downtime events with a specific reason code for breakdown, material shortage, or QC wait
items.tip: Start with 5 reason codes maximum. You can add more once operators actually use them

items.text: Pull a 30-day report of actual vs. estimated production time for any job without asking three people
items.tip: If this takes more than 10 minutes to generate, your reporting layer is the bottleneck

sections.title: Data and Systems of Record
sections.description: Figure out where your truth lives, who owns it, and how many duplicate versions are floating around in spreadsheets and inboxes.

items.text: Identify one system of record for orders, inventory, and production data
items.tip: If two systems disagree, decide today which one wins and enforce it

items.text: Inventory every shadow spreadsheet or personal tracker your team relies on to do their job
items.tip: Ask each supervisor to screen-share their daily workflow. The hidden sheets always appear

items.text: Assign a named owner for each critical dataset so accuracy has a responsible human
items.tip: No owner means no accountability. Put the name on the system, not just in a meeting

sections.title: People and Handoffs
sections.description: Most ops problems are handoff problems. Audit where work changes hands, stalls, or depends on one irreplaceable person.

items.text: Document every handoff between sales, production, QC, and shipping with clear trigger events
items.tip: Draw the handoff map on one page. If it needs two pages, it is already too complex

items.text: Flag any process that breaks if one specific person is out for a week
items.tip: Run a dry test by having that person take an unannounced day. Watch what breaks

items.text: Write a one-page SOP for your five most frequent recurring jobs
items.tip: Record a 3-minute Loom of the person doing it today and transcribe. That is your first draft

items.text: Maintain a cross-training matrix showing who can cover which role in an emergency
items.tip: Color-code gaps in red. Solving the reddest gap first usually has the highest payoff

completion_cta.headline: You Have the Map. Now Fix in the Right Order.
completion_cta.description: Review every item you flagged and rank them by impact and effort. Start with one high-impact fix before touching any new software or automation, because automating a broken process only makes the pain faster.
